Make /llms.txt Return text/markdown on GitHub Pages: A Cloudflare Transform Rule Guide

I dropped /llms.txt onto my gh-pages site, ran curl -I, and found it coming back as text/plain. This post walks through fixing that with a single Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule — no Worker needed, free plan covers it. Covers why .txt defaults to text/plain via mime-db, the free plan's 10-rule allowance, the full Dashboard setup, how to write the match expression, and verifying with curl -I.
Cloudflare Pages Preview URLs Stay Live Forever? Lock Them Down with Cloudflare Access

Every Cloudflare Pages deployment gets a hash-based preview URL like `d682e28c.your-project.pages.dev`. This post starts with the security problem of those URLs persisting forever, then walks through how to use Cloudflare Access (free for up to 50 users) to put a Self-hosted application policy in front of them — locking the previews behind an "Emails ending in" rule. Covers the step-by-step setup, how to write OR rules in Access policies, and three things to check when the One-time PIN email doesn't arrive.
From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready's AI Agent Readiness Test

I ran my blog through Cloudflare's isitagentready.com and got a brutal 3/100. Here's how I fixed every single item, one by one, all the way to a perfect score — code, screenshots, and the gotchas I hit along the way.
Migrating from Firebase Storage to Cloudflare R2 — the Full Move

Earlier I wrote about caching Firebase Storage images with Cloudflare Workers, but in the end caching is just a layer in front — the images still live on Firebase and the bill still runs. So I ended up moving everything off the two Firebase Storage buckets onto Cloudflare R2 — nearly 4800 images, 2200+ article links handled in one shot. This is the full migration log, including the gotchas and the final architecture.
Cache Firebase Storage Images with Cloudflare Workers — Completely Free Bandwidth Savings

If you're using Firebase Storage to host blog images like I was, you've probably noticed bandwidth costs creeping up — every article view pulls the images straight from Firebase Storage, and all of it lands on your Firebase bill. This post walks through how to use Cloudflare Workers plus a custom subdomain to cache those images. The whole setup is free and doesn't require a credit card.
Buy a Domain (Google Domains) and Point It at Your Blog on GitHub Pages With Cloudflare

Heads up — Google Domains was discontinued in 2023 and transferred to Squarespace. The Google Domains-specific parts of this post are historical, but the GitHub Pages + Cloudflare setup is still applicable. If you're setting up a new domain today, see my newer post on Cloudflare Domain instead. Keeping this post as historical reference for anyone who already has a Google Domain.
